title: ASU Scholarship Search
summary: Scholarship searching works best when you stop treating it like one big perfect application and start treating it like a repeatable filter-and-apply process.

modes.label: Online
modes.summary: Use the ASU scholarships site and filters to narrow the list, then open the opportunities that actually fit you.

steps.title: Start from the ASU scholarships page
steps.description: Begin with ASU's scholarship overview so you are using the right search entry point, not guessing from old links.
bullets: Look for college-specific, merit, need-based, and profile-based opportunities.
Use your real student details when filtering.

visual.title: Filter first, then read details
visual.caption: The goal is not to open every scholarship. The goal is to find the short list that actually fits you.

steps.title: Filter honestly and check deadlines early
steps.description: The two biggest time-savers are filtering by your actual profile and checking the deadline before you read deeply.
bullets: Filter by year, major area, residency, GPA, and first-gen status if relevant.
Open only the scholarships that still fit after filtering.
Write down the deadline before you spend time on the application.
steps.reassurance: Students often self-reject before the scholarship system rejects them. Let the criteria decide, not your anxiety.

visual.title: Eligibility and deadline before effort
visual.caption: A fast no is useful. It saves your time for the better fits.

steps.title: Apply in small batches instead of one giant session
steps.description: Scholarship searching gets easier when you treat it like a weekly batch task instead of a once-a-semester panic event.
bullets: Save links, deadlines, and required materials in one note.
Submit the strongest fits before hunting for more.

visual.title: Shortlist, track, apply
visual.caption: Consistency beats one heroic late-night search.

modes.label: In person
modes.summary: Use the scholarship search online first, then get in-person help with narrowing options or understanding application requirements.

steps.title: Do one quick search before asking for help
steps.description: In-person help is much more useful if you arrive with a few scholarships or one confusing requirement already identified.
bullets: Run a basic search first using your year, major, and profile.
Bring the deadline or requirement that feels most confusing.

visual.title: Bring a shortlist, not a blank screen
visual.caption: Help gets better when there is something concrete to point at.

steps.title: Ask whether these scholarships are realistic fits
steps.description: The best use of a support person is helping you prioritize the opportunities that are worth your time.
bullets: Ask which opportunities seem strongest for your profile.
Ask what materials are most important for each one.
Ask whether there are other ASU-specific scholarships you should search next.
steps.reassurance: You are not wasting anyone's time by asking which options are worth pursuing. That saves time for everyone.

visual.title: Get clarity on fit, not vague motivation
visual.caption: The question is not “Do I deserve scholarships?” It is “Which ones should I actually spend time on?”

steps.title: Leave with a ranked list and next application dates
steps.description: Even a short help session is useful if you leave knowing which scholarship to open first tonight.
bullets: Write down the top scholarship to apply for first.
Save the next 1-2 deadlines in your calendar.
Note any missing materials you need before applying.

visual.title: One scholarship to start with tonight
visual.caption: Specificity matters more than motivation here.

questionSet.defaultOpener: Hi, I want to make sure I am spending time on scholarships that actually fit me.
questionSet.defaultProTip: If you find a scholarship that might fit, save the deadline before you do anything else.
questionSet.firstTimeIntro: A lot of students assume scholarships are only for a tiny group of perfect applicants. That assumption blocks more people than the actual criteria do.

scenarioPlaceholders.class: School is already expensive and I want to know whether there are scholarship options I might actually fit.
scenarioPlaceholders.money: I am worried about costs and I need help figuring out which scholarships are worth applying for.
scenarioPlaceholders.overwhelmed: I know I should be searching for scholarships, but I do not know how to narrow the list down.
scenarioPlaceholders.schedule: I need a realistic scholarship plan that fits around class and work.
scenarioPlaceholders.something-else: I want help turning the scholarship search into something concrete instead of vague.

first-year: Which scholarships are the strongest fit for a first-year student like me?
Are there first-gen or profile-based scholarships I might be missing?
What should I look at first: eligibility, deadline, or required materials?
What is one scholarship question students usually forget to ask?
How do I keep this from turning into an overwhelming search?
second-year: Which scholarships fit my current year and profile best?
How should I prioritize scholarships if I only have time for a few applications?
Are there college-specific scholarships I should search differently now?
What materials should I gather before I start applying?
What deadline should I be planning around next?
third-year: Are there scholarships tied to upper-division standing, major progress, or internships that I should know about?
Which scholarship filters matter most for someone at my stage?
How do I avoid wasting time on low-fit opportunities?
What is one question that helps me understand the real competitiveness of a scholarship?
What should I apply for first if I need to be practical about time?
fourth-year-plus: Which scholarships are still realistic and useful at this stage of my degree?
What should I prioritize if I need funding help without spending weeks searching?
Are there deadline or eligibility details that matter more for later-year students?
How do I know which applications are worth finishing first?
What is the next smartest scholarship step from here?
